extracted_rules.resource: Right to refuse unjust commands
proof: [§2, §9] For since the gods laid constraining doom about my city and led me from my father’s house to a slave’s lot, it is fitting for me to govern my bitter hate, even against my will, and submit to the wishes of my masters, whether just or unjust.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Chorus, identifying as enslaved women captured from their father's house, explicitly describes the social and legal requirement of their status: they must obey their masters regardless of the moral quality ('just or unjust') of the command, effectively losing the right to personal agency or moral refusal.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire class of enslaved persons within the social hierarchy described by the author.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement within the home
proof: [§2, §4] Terror, the diviner of dreams for our house, breathing wrath out of sleep, uttered a cry of terror in the dead of night from the heart of the palace, a cry that fell heavily on the women’s quarter.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of a 'women’s quarter' (gynaikonitis) indicates a formal spatial division within the Greek household where women were expected to remain, reflecting a customary restriction on their movement compared to men.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all women living within significant households in this society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to perform burial rites
proof: [§1] For I was not present, father, to lament your death, nor did I stretch forth my hand to bear your corpse.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Orestes' lamentation over his absence implies a specific legal and customary obligation for the son/male heir to personally handle the corpse and lead funeral lamentations, a core resource of lineage and family standing.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to specific family members (heirs) rather than a broad general population.

extracted_rules.resource: Commonality of mortal destiny
proof: [§10] For the portion of fate awaits both the free man and the man enslaved by another’s hand.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Electra explicitly states that spiritual or fatal consequences apply equally to the enslaved as they do to the free, suggesting a shared moral or cosmic standing despite their status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general philosophical/legal principle applied to the most broad categories of the population (free and slave).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit ancestral property
proof: [§10] As for me, I am no better than a slave, Orestes is an outcast from his inheritance, while they in their insolence revel openly in the winnings of your toil.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes Orestes, the legitimate son, being 'an outcast' from his inheritance, which is treated as a violation of the rule that he should possess his father's estate and 'winnings of toil'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to the heirs of royal or noble houses in the context of succession.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in communal libations
proof: [§10] And the god declared that to such as these it is not allowed to have a part either in the ceremonial cup or in the cordial libation; his father’s wrath, though unseen, bars him from the altar; no one receives him or lodges with him; and at last, despised by all, friendless, he perishes, shrivelled pitifully by a death that wastes him utterly away.
extracted_rules.reasoning: According to the oracle described by Orestes, a person exiled/cursed for kindred blood is legally and religiously barred from communal drink-offerings and altars.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific legal/social class (those under hereditary blood-guilt or exile).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive lodging
proof: [§10] no one receives him or lodges with him; and at last, despised by all, friendless, he perishes, shrivelled pitifully by a death that wastes him utterly away.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines the social exclusion of the cursed exile, where they are denied the material resource of shelter/lodging by the rest of society.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes the status of any individual falling under specific religious/legal bans.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reciprocal justice
proof: [§10] Justice cries out as she exacts the debt, and for a murderous stroke let a murderous stroke be paid. Let it be done to him as he does, says the age-old wisdom.
[§11] Justice cries out as she exacts the debt, and for a murderous stroke let a murderous stroke be paid. Let it be done to him as he does, says the age-old wisdom.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites 'age-old wisdom' as a rule that those who suffer violence have the legal/customary right to see equal violence inflicted upon the perpetrator (life for life).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is presented as a fundamental law of the society (tit-for-tat) applicable to all murder cases.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to exact retributive justice
proof: [§24] And it is the eternal rule that drops of blood spilled on the ground demand yet more blood. Murder cries out on the Fury, which from those killed before brings one ruin in the wake of another.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies an 'eternal rule' of lex talionis (law of retaliation) where murder necessitates a reciprocal killing to satisfy the 'Fury'. This establishes a customary legal framework where the survivors are empowered and expected to pursue violent retribution for a slain kinsman.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any family member of a murdered person, a significant but specific subset of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public mourning and funeral attendance
proof: [§29] You dared to give your husband a most cruel burial: unmourned, without lamentation, a king unattended by his people.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Electra's outrage at Agamemnon's burial 'unattended by his people' reveals a customary rule that a king is entitled to a public funeral with formal lamentation by the citizenry. The violation of this rule is cited as a specific 'dishonor'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies only to the very small group of individuals holding royal office.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public admiration and good name
proof: [§17] Then you would have left a good name for your children in their halls, and in their maturity you would have made their lives admired by men.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Orestes notes that the honorable death of a father (specifically a king/general) confers a 'good name' and 'admiration' upon the children, indicating that social capital and prestige are resources transferred through patrilineal descent.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the offspring of the socio-political elite.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement and social participation
proof: [§32] But all the while I was kept sequestered, despised, accounted a worthless thing. Kennelled in my room as if I were a vicious cur, I gave free vent to my streaming tears... as in my concealment I poured out my lament.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Electra describes being 'sequestered' and 'kennelled' in her room. This reflects a social practice (intensified here by political enmity) of restricting the movement of high-born women to the interior of the house, denying them a public presence.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to unmarried daughters of high-status households.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in ancestral home
proof: [§25] behold the remnants of the line of Atreus in their helpless plight, cast out from house and home in dishonor.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the status of exiles (Orestes and Electra's marginalized state) as being 'cast out from house and home'. This indicates that the loss of civic standing resulting in exile entails the concrete loss of the right to occupy or possess one's family estate.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to individuals who have been formally or effectively banished from their city-state.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to lordship and ancestral halls
proof: [§39] Orestes O father, who perished by a death unbefitting a king, grant in answer to my prayer the lordship over your halls!
extracted_rules.reasoning: Orestes, as the son of the deceased king, prays for the 'lordship over your halls,' reflecting the Athenian and Greek custom of patrilineal inheritance where the male heir claims the right to the father's estate and authority.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to male heirs of propertied or royal families, a significant but specific sub-group of the citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to an inheritance portion for marriage
proof: [§39] Electra And I will likewise at my wedding offer libations to you out of the fullness of my inheritance from my father’s house
extracted_rules.reasoning: Electra refers to her 'inheritance' specifically in the context of her wedding, which aligns with the Greek custom of the dowry or the portion of the paternal estate reserved for daughters.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies generally to daughters within families with property.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to legal and social continuity
proof: [§39] Have compassion on your offspring, on the woman and on the man as well, and let not this seed of Pelops’ line be blotted out: for then, in spite of death, you are not dead. For children are voices of salvation to a man, though he is dead
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights a societal rule where a man's legal and social existence (his 'salvation') is preserved after death through his surviving children, who act as his agents in the world of the living.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general cultural and legal principle applying to the heads of households.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to act with audacity or public agency
proof: [§40, §45] But I honor the hearths of homes not heated by passion’s fires, and in woman a spirit that shrinks from audacious deeds.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The chorus explicitly praises the 'spirit that shrinks from audacious deeds' in women as a social norm, contrasting it with the 'reckless passions' that lead to social ruin. This indicates a customary restriction on independent female agency.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general gender-based behavioral norm applying to all women.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be received by a householder
proof: [§39] Why then does Aegisthus have his door shut on his suppliant, if in fact he is at home and knows?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Orestes anticipates that keeping a door shut against a suppliant is a breach of social and religious etiquette, implying that a suppliant has a recognized right to at least be heard or received.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Being a suppliant is a specific temporary status anyone might find themselves in, but it characterizes a distinct legal/religious interaction.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to lead serious discourse
proof: [§49] Tell some one to come forth who has authority over the house, the mistress in charge. But the master would be more fitting, for then no delicacy in speaking makes words obscure: man speaks boldly to man and reveals his meaning without reserve.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Orestes explicitly states that while a mistress has authority, a male master is 'more fitting' for serious or direct communication. This reflects a social rule where women are excluded from or marginalized in 'bold' or transparent diplomatic/legal speech, which is reserved for men.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all free women of status in the society when dealing with guest-host relations and serious news.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to deliberate on grave matters
proof: [§49] But if there is another matter requiring graver counsel, that is the concern of men, and we will communicate with them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clytaemestra acknowledges a social boundary where 'graver counsel' (likely referring to political, legal, or military matters) is the exclusive 'concern of men,' effectively barring women from participating in high-level decision-making.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general social norm regarding the division of labor/authority by gender in Athenian-era Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to lodging and provisions
proof: [§49] Strangers, you have only to declare your need, for we have everything that suits this house: warm baths, beds to charm away fatigue, and the presence of honest faces.

[§49] But it is the hour when strangers who have been travelling on a long day’s journey should have their proper entertainment. To an attendant Conduct him to the rooms where the men are hospitably lodged, him and his attendants here and his fellow-traveller, and let them be tended to there as is proper in our house.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines the customary right of 'Xenia' (guest-friendship), where strangers/foreigners are entitled to concrete material resources (baths, beds, food) and lodging simply by virtue of their status as travelers.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to anyone traveling from another land/city-state.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from strict liability in service
proof: [§49] Conduct him to the rooms where the men are hospitably lodged, him and his attendants here and his fellow-traveller, and let them be tended to there as is proper in our house. I command you to do this as you shall be held to strict account.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mistress holds the attendants to 'strict account' for the care of guests, implying a legal or customary vulnerability where servants face punishment or liability if the material needs of guests are not met.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to domestic servants (likely enslaved) tasked with hospitality duties.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to rest and singular task focus
proof: [§49] ...and the many and troublesome tasks, fruitless for all my enduring them, when his loud and urgent cries broke my rest.... For one must nurse the senseless thing like a dumb beast, of course one must, by following its humor. ... Yet many a time, I think, having to wash the child’s linen because of my own errors, laundress and nurse had the same function.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Nurse’s description confirms that those in the occupation of 'nurse' were required to perform multiple forms of manual labor (laundry and childcare) without the right to undisturbed rest, treating their labor as a multi-functional resource for the household master.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is specific to a professional/status subgroup within the servant class.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to personal interrogation of witnesses
proof: [§61] Aegisthus: I wish to see the messenger and put him to the test again—whether he himself was present at the death or merely repeats from vague reports what he has heard. No! Be sure he cannot deceive a mind with eyes open.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aegisthus, as the male head of the household, asserts a right to conduct his own interrogation ('put him to the test') to verify evidence, contrasting his 'eyes open' inquiry with 'reports spread by women' which he dismisses as unreliable.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a power held by heads of households or men in authority regarding the verification of news and legalistic testimony.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit realm and ancestral property
proof: [§61] Chorus: Or else, kindling a flaming light in the cause of freedom, Orestes will win both the rule over his realm and the rich possessions of his fathers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies Orestes' victory as the means by which he will regain the 'rule over his realm' and the 'possessions of his fathers,' reflecting the Athenian and broader Greek legal norm of patrilineal succession for legitimate sons.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Inheritance laws for sons were a foundational element of Greek society affecting all land-owning families.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement outside the home
proof: [§61] Orestes: Do not accuse him who suffered while you sat idle at home. ... Aegisthus: Yes, but it is the husband’s toil that supports them while they sit at home.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The dialogue between Orestes and Clytaemestra highlights the social rule/norm that women remain within the domestic sphere ('sit at home') while men engage in external 'toil' or warfare, framing the woman's presence at home as an obligation or state of idleness compared to male public activity.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This reflects a general gender-based stratification of space common in Classical Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution by children
proof: [§61] Clytaemestra: Take care: beware the hounds of wrath that avenge a mother. ... Orestes: Pylades, what shall I do? Shall I spare my mother out of pity?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clytaemestra invokes a specific customary/religious protection ('the hounds of wrath' or Furies) that penalizes matricide. Orestes' hesitation shows that killing a mother is a breach of a profound social and sacred rule, distinct from ordinary homicide.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While mother-child relations are universal, the specific legal/religious protections against matricide are a distinct category of blood-guilt rules.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from being sold into slavery
proof: [§61] Orestes: I was sold in disgrace, though I was born of a free father.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Orestes identifies his being 'sold' as a 'disgrace' specifically because he was born of a 'free father.' This implies a societal rule that children of free-born citizens are protected from being treated as commodities or sold into foreign service/slavery.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire class of free-born individuals in the polis.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution
proof: [§72] For I do not speak of Aegisthus’ death: he has suffered the penalty prescribed for adulterers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Orestes notes that the death of Aegisthus is a standardized legal or customary consequence ('the penalty prescribed') for the act of adultery, indicating that men who commit adultery lose their right to bodily integrity/life under Athenian-influenced norms.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While adultery is a behavior, the text refers to a 'prescribed penalty' which functions as a general legal rule for a specific class of offenders within the male population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to religious asylum
proof: [§72] And now observe me, how armed with this branch and wreath I go as a suppliant, an outcast for the shedding of kindred blood, to the temple set square on the womb of the earth, the precinct of Loxias, and to the bright fire said to be imperishable.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the formal status of a 'suppliant' and 'outcast' (exile) who, by carrying specific rituat objects (branch/wreath), gains the right to approach a sacred precinct for protection/cleansing after a killing.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual who becomes an 'outcast' or 'suppliant' due to homicide, a significant and defined legal category in Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement within the land
proof: [§72] I go forth a wanderer, estranged from this land, leaving this repute behind, in life or death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Even when a killing is framed as 'just,' the slayer of kindred blood must leave the land as an 'estranged' wanderer, establishing that such individuals lose their right to reside in their home territory.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Exile was a standard legal and social consequence for homicide in the author's society.
